replies.author: @TheOnlyWayOutIzIN
replies.text:
​@SioxerNikitaAre you being deliberately obtuse, or is your need to be right (even when you're wrong) an actual addiction?

The saying refers to one's intrinsic character! 

Not one's body which obviously changes as in your disingenuous "children" "teenager" example and not one's personality which means mask from the Greek "persona"

Yes, people's personalities/masks change over time. In fact, people's personalities/mask can change multiple times a day because we wear different masks when we are with our parents than we do with our friends or work colleagues or significant others.

What the Russian commenter was referring to is that most people don't GROW as that is the only way your intrinsic character can change.

If you are like most modern women - self-absorbed, entitled and selfish - when you are young, you will almost certainly be self-absorbed, entitled and selfish when you are a 55 year old cat lady, because very very few modern women grow/grow up.

So, although there are exceptions, they do not disprove the rule. 

In general, money and fame reveal character and in general, character does NOT change.

Not understanding the difference between personality and character marks you out as one who sees themselves as a deep thinker when the are actually quite a shallow one.

replies.text:
People forget what Marvel was trying to do with 'Catpain Marvel'.  They had just put out 'Black Pander' and had discovered that identity politics can sell and wanted to repeat the experience but for XX people.    It wasn't JUST that she was the 'first female MCU star'... they very explicitly hired ONLY XX people for EVERY SINGLE ROLE in the movie because they were trying to make a point to the activists (just as they had with 'Black Pander').  Only this was a big problem.  XX seldom write action superhero movies, so they had to go through a half-dozen writers producing a mess of a script.  XX seldom direct action superhero movies, so they had to hire one whose husband could secretly help her out on the sly.  And much to their disappointment, very few XX people want or are suited to holding heavy cameras and microphones for hours.

The crown jewel of the movie would be an XX starlet who would be an outspoken activist.  This was EXACTLY what they wanted.  It didn't have to be BREEE... but WHOMEVER they hired was going to be that.  The movie was activism from start to finish.  And this was underscored by both the script and director as the main actress was told to be unemotional and powerful in all her scenes.  They WANTED a plank-like performance.  And, of course, anyone who didn't support her was cast as an XX-hater from the first moment.

You're right that the movie by itself didn't necessarily break her career... but BREEE would never have been cast in it if she were not behaving the way she was.  And everyone surrounding it egged on the very worst parts of her character, making her even more so.  It took her years of humiliation to recover.

author: @TheOwl12345
text: Brie Larson and Rachel Zegler often reflect a broader trend among younger people in the Western world: a strong confidence in their views that can overshadow a willingness to listen or accept that others might know more. Their personalities sometimes come across as prickly—quick to take offense and often filtering complex issues through personal grievances or emotion. Combine that with an eagerness to adopt whatever social stance is currently popular, and the result can feel more like reactive posturing than thoughtful conviction. Passion is admirable—but humility, emotional maturity, and a willingness to engage with other perspectives are what really build respect.

I never questioned Rachel’s talent. I pointed out how certain public statements and attitudes reflect a wider cultural trend. If that feels threatening, it might be worth asking why.

replies.author: @lonelocustoftheapocalypse3700
replies.text: Counterpoint: not the ONLY reason, though. Around then, there was a huge chunk of the moviegoing public who were awaiting a good superhero movie with a woman as a main character - a Sigourney Weaver Ripley in a cape, if you will, or even a Kate Beckinsale from "Underworld." Hell, count me in that crowd, for that matter. The fact that "Captain Marvel" was emphatically NOT that movie is something that wasn't clear before anybody had really had a chance to see it or digest it. The fact that even "Wonder Woman," which in retrospect was decidedly mid-tier until the final act yanked even THAT accomplishment out from under it, had such a huge positive response indicates there was really a market for that sort of thing around that time. If the film had been better-written and better-cast - say, if it would have even reached the "adequate-plus" level of a "Doctor Strange" - it would have been hailed as a masterpiece. Somewhere, there's an alternate universe where the role would have gone to an "Edge of Tomorrow"-era Emily Blunt, using a blood-soaked script found on the floor of a tavern after Peak Joss Whedon and Peak Quentin Tarentino knifed each other to death in an argument over revisions.

replies.text: ​​​@sharingiscaring1952No offense, but that's a bit of a pessimistic take. Anyone can say stupid things when their self esteem skyrockets in such a short amount of time. In other words, an unchecked ego can lead one to act obnoxiously. Brie Larson wasn't that much of an established actress years before Captain Marvel and her Oscar-winning performance role. That sudden rise must've made her a bit arrogant, and weirdly insecure when people teased her. While I am in no way someone who gained fame like her, I know what it's like to feel like you're the best at something that other people doubted about you for a long time, and eventually suffering the consequences out of that arrogance. I'm not excusing that cringe behavior, but people like Critical Thinker adopt this overly cynical attitude towards specific celebrities and ironically become more annoying than those they make fun of. Saying she's controlling her output until she's comfortable again is absurd. I don't think she's that foolish to shoot herself in the foot even further, assuming she has an agent or someone who advises her to keep her image clean.

replies.author: @maverick77defender
replies.text:
So, it's perhaps not surprising that Gal Gadot has strong opinions on the topic of feminism.

The actress - who had almost given up on her film career when she landed Hollywood's most badass female role - believes anyone who is not a feminist is by default sexist.

"People always ask me, 'Are you a feminist?'" Gadot tells Rolling Stone, in a new interview out this month.

"And I find the question surprising, because I think, 'Yes, of course. Every woman, every man, everyone should be a feminist. Because whoever is not a feminist is a sexist.'"
